fact: July 31 is the 212th day of the year, which means there are exactly 153 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically peak summer heat time - the sun has been baking the ground for weeks, making July 31 one of the hottest days of the year in many places.
Cicadas are usually screaming their loudest right now! These buzzing insects often make the most noise possible around late July in warm climates.
Fireflies often seem extra magical on July 31 because the warm, humid summer air helps them glow brighter and fly longer into the night in many regions.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 31 falls smack in the middle of winter - some places might even have snow!
Sunflowers are typically towering giants by July 31, often reaching their full height of 6 to 10 feet tall and turning their massive heads to follow the sun across the sky.
The sun sets later on July 31 than it did on July 1, but here's the weird part - it's already starting to set earlier than it did in mid-July in the Northern Hemisphere!
Mosquitoes are often at their most annoying peak around July 31 because they've had all summer to breed in warm, stagnant water.
July 31 marks the end of the zodiac sign Leo's first week - people born on this day are supposedly extra dramatic and love being the center of attention.
In many northern regions, July 31 is when wild berries like blackberries and blueberries are typically perfectly ripe and ready to stain your fingers purple.
The air often shimmers with heat mirages on July 31 in hot climates, making distant roads look like they're covered in water when they're bone dry.
Bats are usually having a feast around July 31 because there are more flying insects buzzing around in the warm summer air than any other time of year.
